candidates.name: Greg Abbott
candidates.notes: Three-term governor running for fourth term in 2026. Signed Texas Heartbeat Act (SB8) and constitutional carry. Launched Operation Lone Star border initiative. Signed school voucher legislation. Strong NRA supporter (A+ rating). Signed In God We Trust display law for schools. Paralyzed below waist since 1984 jogging accident. [2026 actions: Operation Lone Star 2.0 — shifted from pure border ops to interior immigration enforcement with ICE (~100 arrests/week on TX-Mexico border). Signed SB 8 + SB 36 expanding border-security coordination with federal gov + Homeland Security Division within TX DPS. Legislature earmarked $3.4B for border security in current 2-year budget. Confirms perfect border_immigration score.]

profile.education: B.A. Finance, University of Texas at Austin (1981); J.D., Vanderbilt University Law School (1984)
profile.career: Texas Supreme Court Justice; Texas Attorney General (2002-2015); Governor since 2015
profile.family: Wife Cecilia Abbott (first Hispanic First Lady of TX); daughter Audrey
profile.background: Attorney and jurist; paralyzed from the waist down by a falling tree at age 26; Texas Supreme Court justice (1996-2001); Attorney General (2002-2015); became 48th governor in 2015.

claims.text: Signed Texas Senate Bill 8 (the heartbeat bill, May 2021) protecting unborn children from abortion once a heartbeat is detected, and House Bill 1280 (the trigger-ban law) which took effect after Dobbs and prohibits nearly all abortions in Texas. Abbott has been a featured speaker at the Texas Rally for Life, affirming protection of human life from conception.

Launched Operation Lone Star in 2021, deploying the Texas National Guard and Texas Department of Public Safety to the southern border. Signed legislation allocating $1.54 billion for construction of border barriers. In 2023-2024 initiated busing of migrants to Democratic-led cities and deployed razor-wire barriers along the Rio Grande, becoming the national leader in state-level military border enforcement.

Signed House Bill 1927 (September 2021), making Texas a permitless-carry state allowing Texans to carry a handgun without a license or training — one of the largest Second Amendment expansions in Texas history. Abbott has also signed legislation strengthening castle-doctrine protections and consistently vetoed gun-control measures reaching his desk.

candidates.name: Dawn Buckingham
candidates.notes: First-term Land Commissioner (took office Jan 2023), running for re-election 2026. Physician (oculoplastic surgeon). Seventh-generation Texan. As TX State Senator represented SD-24 (2017-2023). Strong pro-life, pro-2A, pro-border security record in Senate. Managing TX public lands, Alamo, Veterans Land Board.

claims.text: Holds an A+ rating from SBA Pro-Life America and has a 100% pro-life voting record in the Senate. Cosponsored the Life at Conception Act and has voted against every pro-abortion bill, affirming personhood from the moment of fertilization.

Holds an A+ rating from the NRA and has been one of the Senate's most vocal opponents of gun-control proposals: led floor opposition to the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, co-sponsored the Respect for the Second Amendment Act (S.840, 118th Congress), and publicly called colleagues who support red-flag laws and assault-weapons bans 'squishes' on the Second Amendment.

A hard-line border hawk who has introduced multiple Build the Wall bills, demanded military deployment to the southern border, and in February 2024 voted against the bipartisan border deal as far too weak — insisting on mandatory removal, physical wall completion, and an end to catch-and-release.

claims.text: Voted consistently to protect the lives of the unborn and to stop forced taxpayer funding of abortion. Supported the largest pro-life legislative victory in two decades by defunding Planned Parenthood of Medicaid dollars for one year through the 2025 reconciliation bill H.R.1 — affirming life from conception as foundational policy.

A consistent border-security hawk who praised the Trump administration's military-backed enforcement push that drove FY2025 Southwest border apprehensions to their lowest level in half a century, and has called open-borders policies an 'election-year stunt' that endangers West Texas communities.

Voted NO on the Democrats' $1.7 trillion, 4,000-page omnibus spending bill in December 2022, calling taxpayer-funded carve-outs 'wrong no matter the price tag' — a record consistent with anti-deficit, balanced-budget principles.

claims.text: Strongly opposes abortion and affirms life from conception; appears on SBA Pro-Life America's House scorecard with a consistent pro-life voting record, and voted for the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act requiring equal medical care for infants born alive after failed abortions.

A border-security hawk representing a Texas Panhandle district on the southern-border frontier; introduced the Reimbursing Border Communities Act of 2025 to compensate local governments for federal border-enforcement costs, and the Strategic Export Controls and Border Security Enhancement Act of 2026 to tighten cross-border security — consistently supporting a fortified physical border.

Ranked 11th most conservative in the entire House (118th Congress) and opposed new firearm restrictions; as a retired U.S. Navy Rear Admiral representing a rural Texas district he has a consistent record of defending unrestricted Second Amendment rights.

claims.text: Holds a 100% pro-life record from SBA Pro-Life America; spoke on the House floor in favor of the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act and voted consistently to protect the lives of the unborn and to defund Planned Parenthood — including through the 2025 reconciliation bill H.R.1 that stripped Planned Parenthood of Medicaid dollars for one year.

In January 2025 reintroduced the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) Act to require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship to register for federal elections; the bill passed the House in 2024 but died in the Senate, and Roy continued to champion it in the 119th Congress as a core election-integrity measure.

In December 2024 opposed raising the U.S. debt ceiling, drawing criticism from President-elect Trump — Roy held firm, consistent with his Freedom Caucus record of resisting uncapped deficit spending and calling for balanced-budget discipline.

claims.text: Endorsed by Reproductive Freedom for All with a perfect pro-choice congressional scorecard; sponsored the Abortion Care Awareness Act of 2024 (H.R.7684), a bill to fund public health campaigns promoting abortion access and countering what it terms 'abortion stigma' — rejecting any legal recognition of life or personhood from conception.

Advocates for a federal ban on assault-style semi-automatic weapons, stating that private ownership of such firearms is 'the equivalent of some of these people having a cannon … people literally have almost no chance of surviving when some of these weapons are used' — directly opposing the rubric's rejection of any assault-weapons ban or magazine restriction.

claims.text: Voted consistently to protect the lives of the unborn and to stop forced taxpayer funding of abortion, including supporting the 2025 reconciliation bill H.R.1 that defunded Planned Parenthood of Medicaid dollars for one year. Also publicly called out the FDA for approving a new generic abortion drug — affirming a life-from-conception standard.

Introduced the Birthright Citizenship Act of 2025 (H.R.569) to end the misuse of the 14th Amendment, limiting automatic citizenship to children born to U.S. citizens, lawful permanent residents, or active-duty military — directly opposing the anchor-baby loophole the rubric targets.

claims.text: Cosponsored H.R. 431, the Life at Conception Act (118th Congress, 2023), which would extend 14th Amendment equal-protection guarantees to unborn persons from the moment of fertilization — affirming life-begins-at-conception personhood.

Introduced H.R. 6035, the Second Amendment Restoration Act (119th Congress, 2025), which repeals the Biden-era gun-control provisions inserted into the 2022 Bipartisan Safer Communities Act; the bill is endorsed by the NRA, Gun Owners of America, and National Association for Gun Rights.

Publicly backs deploying U.S. military to the southern border and forcibly deporting illegal immigrants, and supports designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations — a military-and-enforcement border posture.

candidates.name: Chad West
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 with 58.78% over Katrina Whatley; term-limited after 2025-27. Pro-choice on record (endorsed 2022 GRACE Act deprioritizing abortion enforcement; absent for the vote itself). Openly gay; Stonewall Democrats endorsement; Dallas Pride grand marshal. Deviation from progressive expectation: genuinely fiscally hawkish — chaired mayor-backed push to cut spending/taxes, backed FY2025-26 tax-rate cut; publicly anti-defund since 2020 ('it's not happening now') and shepherded budgets funding 350 recruits, though he opposed 2024 Dallas HERO Prop U (4,000-officer mandate) on fiscal grounds shared by the police union. Opposed DPD joining ICE 287(g) (Oct 2025). No findable positions on schools, elections, religious liberty, state-overreach cells, or gun policy — left null.

profile.background: Attorney and small-business owner; Army veteran (Bosnia/Hungary deployments); chairs council Finance Committee. First elected 2019; re-elected May 2025 (58.8%) — final term under Dallas's 4-term limit. Openly gay.

candidates.name: Jesse Moreno
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025; Mayor Pro Tem since June 16, 2025 (annual reselection due ~June 2026). Absent for the 2022 GRACE Act vote but publicly endorsed it. Read the city's 2025 Pride Month proclamation. Deviation from progressive expectation: one of only 3 no votes on the FY2025-26 $5.5B budget, dissenting for deeper spending cuts and a bigger tax-rate reduction; and genuine affirmative pro-police advocacy in 2025 — unveiled the Deep Ellum crime plan (more police, mounted patrol) and pushed to RAISE DPD hiring goals — though he voted to pull $10M DPD overtime in 2021 and opposed 2024 Prop U (mixed record, credited on the recent affirmative advocacy). Opposed DPD joining ICE 287(g) (Oct 2025). Created/championed the 2023 Dallas gun-buyback program (voluntary; matches no specific self-defense cell — noted, left null). Religion/education not found.

candidates.name: Zarin Gracey
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 (~54% over Lightbourne and Sims, no runoff); term ends 2027. Openly Christian public official — X bio: 'Through service to my church and my City, I desire to boldly reflect Christ through out the City of Dallas'; former Concord Church executive pastor; a Dallas LGBT-activist publication flagged him as religiously conservative, and Stonewall Democrats' 2023 endorsement list skipped District 3 (suggestive, not scored). Fiscally frustrated with the Prop U mandate while voting for the 300-officer hiring goal (Feb 2025: 'This silly, silly loop is so extremely frustrating') — said he favors more officers (paraphrase, not credited under the high-bar rule). Voted yes on the unanimous FY2024-25 budget and (unnamed in dissent coverage) the FY2025-26 budget. Attended the Nov 6, 2025 joint-committee session that unanimously killed the $25M DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership (only West absent — joined the unanimous rejection, no individual statement). No findable positions on abortion, marriage/trans, schools, elections, state-overreach cells, or guns — left null.

profile.background: ~16 years in Dallas City Hall finance roles (Debt Manager, Cash & Investment Manager, Director of the Office of Business Diversity 2017-2021); municipal finance at First Southwest Co.; Executive Pastor of Ministries at Concord Church 2021-2023. First elected June 2023 (runoff over Joe Tave); re-elected May 3, 2025 (~54%, no runoff). Married to Erin; two adopted sons. D3 = southwest Dallas/west Oak Cliff.

candidates.name: Maxie Johnson
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: won May 3, 2025 outright (~75%) over Alexander and Hardaman, unseating Carolyn King Arnold; seated June 16, 2025; chaired the joint Public Safety/Government Efficiency committee through the 287(g) fight. Corrected stale background (he is D4 south Oak Cliff, not Pleasant Grove; no runoff). Led the Nov 6, 2025 unanimous committee kill of the $25M DPD-ICE 287(g) partnership with hard quotes: 'We're not gonna support racism, we're not gonna take money cause all money ain't good money' and 'it's hard for me to support any organization that rips apart homes based on skin color.' As DISD trustee, used his work email (Aug 2024) to declare 'I have always believed, and continue to advocate, that public funds belong in public schools,' opposing the Governor's school-choice push — documented contrary on school choice. candidates.name: Kathy Stewart
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: re-elected May 3, 2025 (~92.5%), sworn in June 2025. PARTY FLAG: the 'R' label is unsubstantiated — Dallas council is nonpartisan, she told a candidate questionnaire 'it's essential to remain nonpartisan,' and her endorsements were business/real-estate (TREC PAC, MetroTex Realtors), not partisan; label retained but unverified. Genuine affirmative pro-police record: proposed the 325-officer/yr DPD hiring-goal compromise (Feb 2025), praised the police/fire pension funding deal for ending recruitment 'uncertainty,' championed the neighborhood police officer program (Dec 2025). Opposed Dallas HERO Props S/T/U on budget grounds shared with the police union ('devastating to the city budget... and to police officers who could lose their immunity'). 287(g): issued a neutral statement supporting officers and the chief; joined the unanimous committee postponement that shelved the ICE partnership — left null (declining a voluntary partnership is not a sanctuary policy; individual roll call unverified). Co-signed concerns the 2024 marijuana-decriminalization charter amendment 'appears to be straight-up illegal' (legality concern, not scored). Voted for the FY2025-26 budget with the 10th straight tax-rate cut (routine governance, not scored). No findable positions on abortion, marriage/gender, school choice, religious liberty, elections, state overreach, or guns — left null; sparse denominator noted. Religion not documented.

candidates.name: Bill Roth
candidates.notes: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local tier). Verified current: won June 7, 2025 D11 runoff 53.78%-46.22% over Jeff Kitner; sworn in June 16, 2025; term ends 2027. Declined to state a party ('the seat is, by design, nonpartisan'); endorsed by fiscal-conservative councilmember Cara Mendelsohn. His office won the Amber Guyger murder conviction. The specific failure mode is on life q4 — Soros-funded DAs systematically decline to prosecute attacks on pro-life pregnancy centers and refuse to enforce state abortion restrictions.

profile.background: Mayor since 2021, re-elected May 2025 with 66.5% of the vote. Youngest mayor of a major US city when elected at age 37. Republican, business-friendly, pro-growth, supported by conservative establishment. Former chief of staff to prior mayor.
Re-elected May 2025 with 73.7%. Represents north Fort Worth. Conservative-leaning, long-serving council member.
Re-elected May 2025 with 71.6%. Represents southeast Fort Worth. Conservative, public safety focus.
Re-elected May 2025 with 74.5%. Represents southwest Fort Worth/Benbrook area. Conservative, fiscally responsible.

Elected May 2025 with 54.3%, replacing Gyna Bivens. Former chair of Tarrant County Democratic Party. Retired AT&T executive. Represents east Fort Worth. Ran for mayor in 2021 but lost to Mattie Parker.
Won June 7, 2025 runoff against Daryl Davis II. Assumed office June 17, 2025. Replaced Jared Williams who did not seek reelection. Represents southeast/south Fort Worth.
Re-elected May 2025 unopposed. Represents west Fort Worth. Conservative, neighborhood development focus.
Re-elected May 2025 with 74.1%. Represents far north Fort Worth/Alliance area. Conservative, pro-growth, public safety advocate.
Re-elected May 2025 with 65.6%. Represents south/southwest Fort Worth. Moderate conservative.
Re-elected May 2025 unopposed. Represents far northwest Fort Worth. Conservative.
Re-elected May 2025 with 77.1%. Represents northeast Fort Worth/Haltom City area. Moderate, community-focused.

Tarrant County Criminal DA since Jan 1, 2023. Former judge of County Criminal Court No. 10 for 25 years. Running for re-election. Tough-on-crime philosophy, traditional prosecution approach. Achieved first TX jury conviction under new state law in 2025.

candidates.name: Ken Paxton
candidates.office: U.S. Senate Texas (2026 R Candidate · sitting TX AG · MAGA conservative)
candidates.notes: Sitting Texas Attorney General (2015-present). Survived 2023 impeachment trial in Senate. Trump-aligned. R primary candidate for TX U.S. Senate 2026 (running against Cornyn from the right).

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 TX U.S. Senate challenger. Archetype: maga_conservative_r. · 2026-05-20 — Resolved conflicting-office duplicate (kept verified 2026 race).

claims.text: As TX Attorney General, secured a unanimous Texas Supreme Court ruling upholding the Human Life Protection Act (near-total abortion ban) and won at the U.S. Supreme Court protecting Texas pro-life laws; consistently defends life from conception in court.

As TX AG, successfully sued the Biden ATF and blocked the rule reclassifying pistols with stabilizing braces as short-barreled rifles — stopping a regulation that would have criminalized millions of law-abiding gun owners.

As TX AG, won the federal-court ruling vacating DACA and halted Biden's unlawful freeze on deportations, enforcing mandatory removal of unlawfully present aliens and building the nation's strongest state-level border-enforcement record.

candidates.name: Dan Patrick
candidates.office: Lieutenant Governor of Texas (R incumbent · 2026 reelection · 4th term bid)
candidates.notes: R incumbent Lt Governor of Texas (since 2015, seeking 4th term). Former state senator, talk radio host. One of most powerful Lt Govs in US (controls TX Senate). Seeking 2026 reelection.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 TX state executive race. Archetype: maga_conservative_r. · 2026-05-20 — Deduped batch-suffix record (merged duplicate). · 2026-05-21 — v5.0 new-category seed (conservative lean inferred from existing God First pattern; party-default archetype, not individual evidence).

claims.text: Championed Texas SB 1 (2021), the Texas Election Security Act, which extended mandatory photo voter ID to mail-in ballots, banned drive-through and 24-hour voting, and standardized election rules across all 254 Texas counties — fulfilling the rubric's voter-ID and anti-mass-mail-in standard. In 2025 Patrick steered SB 16 through the Texas Senate to stop non-citizens from voting by mandating citizenship verification in voter rolls.

Led the Texas Senate passage of the Texas Heartbeat Act (SB 8, 2021) — the nation's first effectively enforced abortion ban, prohibiting abortion at approximately six weeks' gestation — and in 2025 made SB 33 (Stopping Taxpayer-Funded Abortion Travel) a top legislative priority, blocking state resources from facilitating out-of-state abortions. Holds a lifetime pro-life record affirming protection of the unborn from conception.

Passed SB 6 (2023) directing Texas state funds to build additional border-wall segments, championed SB 4 (2023) granting Texas state troopers authority to arrest illegal border crossers under state law, and in 2023 passed SB 2424 expanding state authority to enforce immigration law — treating border security as a first-order state constitutional responsibility alongside federal enforcement.

candidates.name: Glenn Hegar
candidates.office: Texas Comptroller (R incumbent · 2026 reelection · former TX state senator)
candidates.notes: R incumbent Texas Comptroller (since 2015). Former TX state senator. Seeking 2026 reelection (4th term). Chief tax collector + financial officer of TX.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 TX state executive race. Archetype: establishment_r. · 2026-05-20 — Deduped batch-suffix record (merged duplicate).

claims.text: As Texas Comptroller, Hegar administered the 2021 state law requiring Texas pension and school funds to divest from financial companies that boycott the fossil-fuel industry. In 2022 he sent letters to more than 100 of the world's largest financial firms demanding they disclose whether they restrict business with energy companies, then blacklisted BlackRock and nine other firms — calling ESG 'an opaque and perverse system' that uses financial clout to 'push a social and political agenda.'
Texas Right to Life awarded Hegar its 'Perfectly Pro-Life Award,' reflecting a consistent record opposing abortion throughout his service in the Texas state legislature and as Comptroller.

Hegar has repeatedly called for sound-money discipline in state finances, opposing unfunded spending commitments, and his Comptroller office maintained the state's biennial revenue forecasts as a firewall against deficit budgeting — a posture consistent with the rubric's preference for fiscal restraint over inflationary government borrowing.

candidates.name: Eric Johnson
candidates.notes: Mayor of Dallas (since 2019). Lifelong Democrat; switched to Republican in 2023 (rare big-city D-to-R mayor switch). Former TX state representative. Term-limited 2027.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — Mayor of Dallas. Archetype: establishment_r.

claims.text: In September 2023 Johnson publicly switched from Democrat to Republican, explicitly citing support for police funding as a primary motivation: progressive protesters outside his home called for defunding the police while Republican officials were the ones who offered solidarity. As Dallas mayor he made public safety and policing a top priority, opposing the 'defund the police' movement.

Johnson cited low property taxes as a core reason for his September 2023 party switch to Republican, signaling a fiscal-restraint and anti-tax-increase orientation consistent with the rubric's balanced-budget and anti-deficit standard.
